title: Egg production records and laying tallies
subtitle: Tracking what your flock is actually producing.
excerpt: A simple daily egg count tells you whether your flock is performing as expected and gives you early warning of disease, moult, or poor nutrition before the visual signs are obvious. A tally board in the coop takes thirty seconds and builds a useful annual picture.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Laying Hens, gov.uk. FAO Small-scale poultry production manual (2004). Lewis Wright, The Practical Poultry Keeper (1867), Internet Archive.

glossaryTerms.definition: The proportion of birds laying on a given day, expressed as a percentage. A healthy hybrid layer flock in peak season should achieve 75 to 90 percent lay rate.
A reduction in daily egg count of more than 20 percent sustained over three or more days. Can indicate stress, disease, nutritional deficiency, moult, or seasonal change.

content.text: A flock that was producing five eggs a day from six hens last week and is now producing two from six hens is telling you something. Without a record, you won't notice the drop clearly until it has persisted for several days. With even a rough tally, the change stands out in a morning.
Date, total eggs collected, number of hens in lay, and any notes (moult starting, new birds introduced, wormed, unusual weather). That is the minimum. From those four columns you can calculate the
for any period and identify any

A laminated tally card on a hook inside the coop, updated with a dry-erase marker when you collect eggs, is the simplest system. Transcribe the weekly total to a notebook or spreadsheet once a week if you want to keep a longer record.
For egg sales under fifty birds direct-to-consumer, UK law does not require production records. But a tally board still earns its keep as a management tool, even if it's never shown to anyone.
Gradual decline over two to four weeks in late September: normal pre-moult reduction. Expect a six-to-eight week low-production period.
Sudden drop of 50 percent or more: check for stress events, fox visit, dog in the run, overnight temperature drop. Also check for respiratory symptoms in the flock.
Steady low production through spring and summer: check the calcium supply, keep the oyster shell grit feeder always full, and rule out a heavy worm burden. Also check the feed quality.
Eggs disappearing without trace: suspect egg eating by the flock, or access by a rat or weasel. A trap nest can identify which bird is eating.

After a year's records you'll have a clear picture of your flock's cycle. Spring brings a surge, summer holds it, autumn drops it, and winter lowers it further. This tells you when to bring in new birds, whether to run a heat lamp, and whether your flock is producing what the breed should.

A first-year laying hybrid produces well. By year two, production drops by around 20 percent. By year three, 30 to 40 percent below peak. The annual records tell you when to consider rotating in new birds rather than carrying an increasingly unproductive flock through winter.
